explanation: IB2-116 requires that the Image Widget be resizable using the Nav panel Width slider ("Be able to set the ratio of the Image to change the ‘Block'’s width..." and "Be able to still resize the Widget by dragging the dot line"). IB2-308 explicitly forbids changing the Image Widget width from the Nav panel ("The Image Widget's width must not be changeable from the Nav Panel Width slider" and "Users must resize the Image Widget only by dragging the dotted outline directly on the page"). These are mutually exclusive requirements for the same control and widget behavior; both cannot be implemented as-written.
suggested_fix: Clarify product intent: choose one approach (Nav Width slider OR direct in-page dragging) or define conditions when each applies (e.g., global toggle/MCP setting, per-template setting, or a control that syncs both input methods). Update both stories to reflect the chosen behavior and any UI/UX syncing rules (e.g., if slider exists it updates the in-page handle and vice versa).

explanation: IB2-117 requires that after resizing the image the image remains in the same percentage position within the Block and preserves that proportional placement if the Block width changes. IB2-308 explicitly requires that when the Image Widget is resized, the image should reset to the center of its Block instead of preserving the same horizontal percentage position. These are mutually exclusive behaviors for the same Image Widget repositioning/resizing flow.
suggested_fix: Clarify the intended UX: decide whether resizing preserves proportional placement or resets to center. If both behaviours are needed in different contexts, add clear acceptance criteria that specify when each applies (e.g., a mode toggle, a per-widget setting, or behavior only for specific operations). Update both stories to reflect the chosen behavior and adjust tests accordingly.

explanation: IB2-15 states that when no end-user custom configs are enabled (and no workflow on the parent) the end user sees no overlay and child records are created immediately after the parent is saved (plus a snackbar confirming creation). IB2-20 states that in scenarios where end-user config does not display ("no end user customisation is enabled") and the child Database's title field has been changed to a non-text field (a 'broken' config), then no child records are created and no snackbar error messaging is needed. These two stories prescribe opposite outcomes for the same observable condition (no end-user config displayed): IB2-15 -> create child records; IB2-20 -> do not create child records when the child DB has been edited. Without a clear precedence or explicit condition (i.e., whether IB2-15 only applies when the child DB form is valid), implementing both as-written is contradictory.
suggested_fix: Clarify and reconcile the acceptance criteria: explicitly state that IB2-15 applies only when the child Database's form/Title field is valid (e.g., 'title is a valid text field'), and that IB2-20 documents the exception for a 'broken' child form where child creation must be suppressed. Add an acceptance test case for the broken-child-DB scenario and an explicit precedence rule in the parent story (or merge the exception behavior into IB2-15) so developers know which outcome to implement.

explanation: IB2-78 requires that when deleting a Server fails, the dialog remains open and a snackbar appears with the error message. IB2-172 requires that when deletion fails, the confirmation dialog must close automatically and no snackbar error message should be displayed after a failed delete. These two requirements are mutually exclusive for the same action (failed Server delete) and cannot both be implemented as written.
suggested_fix: Clarify the desired UX for failed Server delete and align both stories. Options: (a) Adopt one behavior and update the other story's acceptance criteria accordingly; or (b) make behavior conditional (e.g., configurable via MCP or apply one pattern for Servers and another for other resources) and explicitly document the condition in both stories. Update acceptance criteria with exact steps (dialog open/closed, presence/absence of snackbar) and any audit/logging expectations.

explanation: IB2-189 requires that the image position remain proportional after resizing and that proportional placement be preserved if the Block width later changes. IB2-308 explicitly requires that when the Image Widget is resized, the image should reset to the center of its Block instead of preserving the same horizontal percentage position. These are mutually exclusive behaviors for image positioning on resize.
suggested_fix: Clarify desired UX: choose one behavior (preserve proportional position across resizes and responsive width changes OR reset to center on resize), or make the behavior configurable (e.g., an Image Widget setting: 'Preserve position on resize' ON/OFF). Update both stories to reference the chosen behavior and add acceptance criteria covering edge cases (responsive breakpoints, undo/redo).

explanation: IB2-190 requires that after resizing/repositioning the image, the image keeps "the same percentage horizontally of Image with the Block in mobile, tablet, and desktop modes" (preserve horizontal percentage across responsive modes). IB2-308 explicitly requires that "When the Image Widget is resized, the image should reset to the center of its Block instead of preserving the same horizontal percentage position." These two requirements are mutually exclusive for the same Image Widget behavior.
suggested_fix: Clarify desired canonical behaviour for resizing/repositioning across responsive breakpoints. Options: (a) adopt preserve-across-breakpoints (IB2-190) and remove or adjust IB2-308, (b) adopt reset-to-center (IB2-308) and update IB2-190, or (c) provide both behaviours as explicit UX options (e.g., a toggle/setting 'preserve focal position across breakpoints') with acceptance criteria and design mocks. Update both stories' acceptance criteria to state which action (resize vs reposition) and which control (drag dots vs Nav width slider) the rule applies to, and add cross-story tests verifying the chosen behaviour across breakpoints.

explanation: IB2-27 defines the Status column's allowed values as: Pending, Approved, Declined, Reviewed, Cancelled. IB2-497 (V2 Database Settings - Feature Field Completion Value) lists approval status options as: Approved, Cancelled, Completed, Declined, Pending. The two stories assert different canonical status labels for approval/workflow state (Reviewed vs Completed). Implementing both as-written would produce inconsistent status vocabularies across the product (lists, settings, mappings), causing UX confusion and possible data-mapping errors.
suggested_fix: Align the canonical set of workflow/approval status values with Product/PM owners. Update one or both stories to use the agreed labels (e.g., replace 'Reviewed' with 'Completed' or vice-versa), and add a mapping/migration note if historical data uses the other label. Ensure acceptance criteria explicitly state the canonical status enum and update related UI components and settings to reference the same enum (or document deliberate differences and their contexts).

explanation: IB2-80 requires that when no Master Templates exist the UI shows 'No Master Templates available' and allows the user to 'Create a Blank Template' as per designs. IB2-337 states the opposite: the 'Start with a blank template' option must only be shown when at least one Master Template exists and when none are available users must NOT be allowed to create a blank template and instead be instructed to create a Master Template first. These are mutually exclusive business rules for the exact same user flow (template creation) and cannot both be implemented as-written.
suggested_fix: Resolve at product/PM level which behaviour is desired. Options: (A) Keep IB2-80 behaviour (allow Create Blank when no Master Templates) and update IB2-337 to match, or (B) Keep IB2-337 (block blank creation until a Master Template exists) and update IB2-80. If both behaviours are needed for different user segments, make the rule explicit (e.g., controlled by an MCP flag or by user role) and update both stories to reference the controlling toggle and acceptance criteria.

explanation: IB2-420 requires the Bulk Review UI to allow a mixed selection (some requests with Hide Decline enabled and some without) and still display Decline as an option (applying it only to applicable items). IB2-455 states that when one Pending request is selected, only checkboxes for relevant related Pending requests remain selectable (i.e., prevent selecting requests that cannot be bulk approved). If IB2-455 is implemented as written, the UI would prevent creating the mixed selection scenario that IB2-420 depends on, making IB2-420's behavior impossible to observe/implement without changing IB2-455.
suggested_fix: Clarify selection model for Bulk Review: either (A) allow mixed selections and implement IB2-420's 'show Decline if any item allows it, apply only to applicable items' behavior, or (B) enforce selection filtering as IB2-455 describes and update IB2-420 to be unnecessary (or convert it into an acceptance criterion describing the filtering behavior). Update both stories' acceptance criteria to state the canonical rule and adjust UI copy/controls accordingly.

explanation: Convert IB2-115 into an explicit clarification / acceptance-criteria task and merge into IB2-305 (keep IB2-305 as the implementation story).
suggested_fix: Consolidate IB2-115 and IB2-305: either merge IB2-115 into IB2-305 (retain IB2-305 as the implementation story) or convert IB2-115 into a clarification/acceptance-criteria ticket that becomes part of the implementation story. Ensure the single resulting story lists the exact widget types (Button, Text, etc.) and concrete acceptance criteria (fields, behaviors) to avoid further overlap.

explanation: IB2-189 and IB2-117 describe the same feature: after placing an Image Widget into a Section show a visual outline + full widget boundary with default 8px spacing, keep the Image Widget outer border visible after resizing, and allow repositioning the image by dragging while preserving proportional placement/percentage when resized or when Block width changes. The acceptance criteria are functionally equivalent; implementing IB2-117 would satisfy IB2-189 entirely.
suggested_fix: Consolidate IB2-189 into IB2-117 (or mark one as duplicate). Merge acceptance criteria and tests into the single story, keep a single implementation and update references to the removed ticket.

explanation: Clarify whether IB2-186 includes Text Widget custom-font support. If yes, close/merge IB2-256; if no, keep IB2-256 scoped as the font-specific implementation.
suggested_fix: Clarify and split responsibilities: update IB2-186 acceptance criteria to explicitly list which Text Widget properties it will deliver (include/exclude custom font support). If IB2-186 will include custom-font support, close or convert IB2-256 into sub-tasks (implementation details/tests). If IB2-256 contains additional UX requirements (font preview, dropdown entries), retain it and narrow IB2-186 to only the Nav-level wiring and generic property surface.

explanation: Both stories require preserving the image's percentage-based horizontal position after resizing and across responsive modes. IB2-189 includes this as part of image positioning rules; IB2-190 focuses specifically on maintaining the same horizontal percentage in mobile, tablet, and desktop after resizing/repositioning. Implementing IB2-189 as written may fully satisfy IB2-190.
suggested_fix: Consolidate or de-duplicate: either merge the responsive-specific acceptance criteria from IB2-190 into IB2-189 (making IB2-189 the single source of truth for image positioning/responsive behavior), or explicitly scope IB2-190 to additional responsive test cases not covered by IB2-189.

explanation: IB2-189 requires displaying a visual outline around the image together with a full Widget boundary using the default 8px spacing. IB2-208 requires selecting the Widget and Block as a whole and highlighting the bounding box as the same experience as the old Block bounding box. Both describe the same bounding-box/selection visual behavior for widgets; implementing a global bounding-box selection (IB2-208) may render IB2-189's first UI requirement redundant for Image Widgets.
suggested_fix: Align UI ownership: decide whether bounding-box selection/highlight is implemented globally (IB2-208) or needs image-widget-specific styling (IB2-189). If global, remove or refactor the Image-Widget-specific outline requirement from IB2-189 and reference the shared selection component; if image-specific styling is required, document the difference (e.g., 8px spacing) in a shared design spec.

explanation: Merge IB2-21 into IB2-240 as an acceptance-criteria item (explicitly require deselected resources not be updated) to avoid duplicated work.
suggested_fix: Clarify ownership: either merge IB2-21 into IB2-240 as an acceptance criterion (explicitly require deselect semantics) or mark IB2-21 as a dependent sub-task of IB2-240. Update acceptance criteria in the parent story to state the exact deselect behavior.

explanation: Add IB2-21's deselect rules as explicit acceptance criteria to IB2-448 and close IB2-21 or mark as dependent subtask to avoid duplicate implementation.
suggested_fix: Either incorporate IB2-21's deselect rule into IB2-448's acceptance criteria, or convert IB2-21 into an explicit sub-task of IB2-448. Product should confirm which story is the source of truth for Bulk Apply selection rules and remove the duplicate scope from the other.

explanation: Both stories request the same core front-end drag-and-drop reordering behavior in the Navigation panel: IB2-215: "Be able to re-order Pages via drag-and-drop from Nav Panel during editing"; "Sub-pages will be moved all together with Page"; "After drag-and-drop ... Sub-pages will be collapsed under Page from Nav Panel." IB2-385: "Allow users to change the order of Pages by using drag-and-drop interactions within the Navigation panel while editing"; "When a Page contains child pages, move all associated sub-pages together with the parent Page"; "ensure all Sub-pages are displayed in a collapsed state beneath their parent." The overlap is a near-identical implementation of DnD reorder + grouped sub-page move + collapse behavior. IB2-385 additionally specifies persistence, migration of references, audit/logging and notifications, but the core UI/FE DnD deliverable is duplicated between the two stories.
suggested_fix: Consolidate the two stories: either merge IB2-215 into IB2-385 (making IB2-385 the parent/encompassing story) or clearly split responsibilities (e.g., IB2-215 = FE-only integration spike for DnD controls; IB2-385 = full end-to-end including persistence, backend updates, audit/logging, notifications). Update acceptance criteria to avoid re-implementing the same DnD behavior in two tickets and add cross-references (link/child tasks) so work is not duplicated.

explanation: IB2-396 (marked '(BE)') repeats the same acceptance steps as IB2-225: 'Be able to click on setting button of a simple page from Nav' and 'Be able to see the sub-page popup'. As written, IB2-396 duplicates UI behavior rather than specifying backend responsibilities (APIs, data model, persistence). If IB2-396 is intended to be a backend task it should not duplicate front-end acceptance criteria.
suggested_fix: Clarify and split responsibilities: if IB2-396 is a BE story, rewrite it to specify API endpoints, request/response contracts, DB changes, and validation rules. Otherwise, merge it into the PB story to avoid duplicated implementation effort.

explanation: IB2-415 has the same title and the same UX steps as IB2-225: both are PB stories titled 'As a User, I want to create a sub-page for an exisiting Smart Page via Nav' and include 'Be able to click on setting button of a simple page from Nav', 'Be able to see the sub-page popup', and 'Be able to input the sub page's title and save'. Completing IB2-225 would make IB2-415 redundant because they ask for the same front-end behavior and UI elements.
suggested_fix: Consolidate IB2-415 into IB2-225 (or vice-versa). Keep a single canonical PB story for the UI work; if separate backend work is required, create a distinct BE story that clearly lists API endpoints and data changes rather than duplicating UI acceptance criteria.

explanation: Both stories request the exact same UI feature: a Left Side Panel (Page Nav) visible when viewing a Simple Page, including the same extend/collapse button from the top-left and showing saved Simple pages based on the current user's permissions. IB2-233: "Be able to see the new extend / collapse button icon from the top left to open the Left Side Panel as Page Nav" and "Be able to see all the saved Simple pages from the Left Side Panel against current user’s permission". IB2-429 contains the same lines. Implementing IB2-233 would make IB2-429's work redundant.
suggested_fix: Merge IB2-429 into IB2-233 (or close IB2-429 as a duplicate). Consolidate any differing acceptance criteria or design details into the single story (ensure unique ACs — e.g., styling/UX notes — are preserved). Assign a single owner and update links/dependencies accordingly.

explanation: IB2-448 and IB2-240 describe the same feature: Bulk Apply for updates introduced through Grouped work. Both require the same UX behaviours ("follow the same behavior as the existing Grouped Review page", "Apply button item counts, tooltip text for enabled and disabled button states, and snackbar notifications"), the same apply processing logic ("Support the same apply processing logic used in the current implementation" vs IB2-240's "Actual apply logic"), additional validation checks, and identical rules for determining Complete Request/Stage based on staged or single requests. Implementing IB2-240 would fully satisfy IB2-448's deliverables, so delivering both would duplicate engineering work.
suggested_fix: Merge IB2-448 into IB2-240 (or vice versa). Consolidate acceptance criteria and validation checks into a single canonical story; keep one story key and close the duplicate. If there are any small differences, enumerate them as explicit sub-tasks or acceptance criteria under the merged story.

explanation: Add IB2-420's mixed-selection Decline rules to IB2-448 acceptance criteria (or explicitly exclude them) to avoid duplication.
suggested_fix: Decide whether IB2-420 is a refinement of IB2-448 or a separate, required behavior. Option: merge IB2-420 into IB2-448 as a named acceptance criterion covering cross-param Decline handling, or update IB2-448 to explicitly include/exclude the Hide-Decline mixed-selection behavior so scope is not duplicated.

explanation: Consolidate checkbox-selection logic into IB2-448 (or mark IB2-455 as a dependent subtask) and update acceptance criteria to prevent overlap.
suggested_fix: Clarify ownership: either merge IB2-455 as a subtask of IB2-448 (so IB2-448 owns implementing the grouped-review selection rules) or update acceptance criteria to explicitly state which story implements checkbox-selection logic and have the other reference it as a dependency.

explanation: Acceptance criteria are incomplete/ambiguous (the 2nd point is truncated and unclear about which collaborators/actions are included). It is unclear exactly which users should see the new action and what constraints apply for staged requests and additional collaborators.
suggested_fix: Clarify and complete acceptance criteria: explicitly list user roles/conditions that should see the action, define behaviour for staged workflows and collaborators not in Requester/Reviewer roles, and include expected UI state and permission checks. Add examples (Gherkin) for common cases.

explanation: The description is vague about scope and expected UI changes (which auth screens, what 'success page' entails, which menus/bars change). References ('CPSL links', 'tiger team consensus') are not actionable for implementation or estimation.
suggested_fix: Specify exact screens and states to update, provide design mocks or reference screens (annotate what 'like the CPSL links' means), and add acceptance criteria (visible elements, responsive breakpoints, accessibility considerations). Add a checklist for required assets and translations if applicable.

explanation: The story does not define the exact scope or behavior of the 'Drag & Drop Page limit' setting: where precisely the limit applies (per page, per site, per account), who can change it (which internal role), default value, validation rules, and whether it is enforced client-side or server-side. Without those details the team cannot reliably estimate effort or design data model/API changes.
suggested_fix: Clarify acceptance criteria: specify the actor/role allowed to change the limit, scope (per page / per tenant / per Custom Page), default and allowed min/max values, where in the MCP UI it must appear (exact Restrictions tab location), whether the setting is persisted server-side and enforced (and how), and add examples of expected behavior and error states.

explanation: The implementation details are ambiguous: 'briefly hide and show' lacks timing/duration/animation specs; 'display according to design' refers to an unspecified design; unclear triggers (only on open, on every re-render, network-failure cases) and accessibility/performance requirements. These gaps prevent confident estimation.
suggested_fix: Provide precise UX specs: animation duration and easing, exact trigger conditions (page open, section view, MCP toggle), whether the animation must run once or every time, accessibility requirements (reduced-motion), performance limits, and attach the design mockups. Add acceptance tests for both success and failure paths.

explanation: The description is incomplete and does not list which Block and Text Widget properties must be combined or how conflicts are resolved. 'Combining previously defined Block and Widget's settings' is vague (which settings, per-widget exceptions, expected UI/UX), so the scope is undefined for estimation.
suggested_fix: Enumerate the specific properties to be surfaced (e.g., padding, margin, background, typography options), provide example widgets showing before/after, clarify if this applies to all widgets or only a subset, and include acceptance criteria for each property (preview, save, inheritance rules). Consider splitting into per-widget or per-property stories if scope is large.

explanation: The acceptance points are confusing and partially contradictory (e.g., 'Module appears if it applies to User’s usage report, whilst in Custom Page usage report the Module will not be displayed'). The dynamic naming placeholder and conditional display rules are unclear, making it impossible to determine required data sources, filters, and UI behavior for reporting.
suggested_fix: Clarify the exact reporting model: define which reports should include the 'Resource Download (Preview)' action, the conditions under which the Module should appear or be hidden, the data schema (fields to record), sample report rows, and filter/search/export behavior. Provide concrete examples and acceptance tests (who sees what, and how aggregation should be handled).

explanation: Renaming a keyword across the front-end is feasible, but the story lacks the comprehensive list of UI locations, content types (labels, help text, URLs, analytics events), and whether translations or docs must be updated. Without that list the work and risk (missed instances) cannot be reliably estimated.
suggested_fix: Provide a complete inventory of locations to be renamed (screens, components, i18n keys, docs), indicate whether backend APIs or analytics events need updates, and include a test checklist to validate no instances are missed. Optionally attach a scriptable search/replace plan for strings files.

explanation: The story defers 'Actual apply logic' to an unspecified source and relies on behavior 'same as Grouped Review' without enumerating rules, edge cases or data model interactions for staged requests. This missing logic and dependency on another feature block accurate estimation and implementation.
suggested_fix: Define the apply rules in detail: map inputs to outcomes, describe how counts/disabled states are computed, list required side effects and API calls, specify behavior for staged requests (how completion is determined), and provide examples of expected UI behavior and error handling. Link to or include the Grouped Review spec that should be matched, and add test cases for common and edge scenarios.

explanation: The story asks to “link the status” of a background image with derived Image resources in the Resource Module but does not define which statuses are in scope, when status linkage should occur (on change, on publish, on load), how conflicts are resolved, or how permissions/error states are handled. Without clear mapping and acceptance criteria the team cannot confidently estimate effort.
suggested_fix: Add precise acceptance criteria: enumerate the statuses to be synchronized, define the exact trigger points (e.g., resource status change -> update page background state in X seconds), include expected UI behavior for success/failure, specify permission rules and edge cases (e.g., missing resource, permission lost), and provide one or two example flows / test cases. If there are performance constraints or async update windows, state them.

explanation: The description is fragmentary and ends abruptly. It references complex cross-folder workflow scenarios (mixes of Global/Local reviewers and differing reviewers per folder) but lacks concrete acceptance criteria, expected outcomes for each scenario, and complete behavior rules. This missing information blocks reliable estimation and planning.
suggested_fix: Complete the story with explicit acceptance criteria and scenario examples. For each cross-folder case, state the expected reviewer selection logic, email/template resolution rules, how conflicts are resolved, and desired failure modes. Provide sample folder+workflow configurations and their expected resulting reviewer lists and notifications. If needed, split into smaller stories by scenario complexity.

explanation: The story requests 'set Page limits on MCP control panel' but does not specify which limits (page count per site, page size, page depth?), expected defaults, enforcement semantics, per-tenant vs global scope, or how limits surface in the UI/API. Without these details the team cannot estimate effort or design enforcement.
suggested_fix: Clarify and enumerate the exact limit types to be supported (e.g., max pages per site, max sections per page, max widgets per page), default values and ranges, whether limits are tenant or global, how violations are enforced (block creation, warning, soft-limit), UI controls required in MCP, and required API/validation changes. Include acceptance criteria and a few example admin workflows (set limit, change limit, effect on existing pages).

explanation: The description is fragmented and ambiguous (incomplete sentences). It does not clearly state the exact behavior change, the affected user roles, or acceptance criteria (e.g., when Requesters can/cannot re-assign, exact logic to exclude V2 Approval List). Team cannot reliably estimate implementation or QA effort from this text alone.
suggested_fix: Rewrite the story with a clear user role, explicit acceptance criteria and examples. Specify: the exact current vs expected behavior, which user roles are allowed to re-assign, UI/API flows impacted, success/failure cases, and any permission checks. Provide a few example scenarios (inputs → expected outputs) and note any performance or security constraints.

explanation: The backend story is underspecified — it only references clicking a settings button and showing a sub-page popup but lacks API/contract details, required fields, expected payloads, permission checks, and success/error behavior. Without those details the team cannot size backend work confidently.
suggested_fix: Add concrete backend acceptance criteria: define the API endpoints, request/response schemas, required fields for creating a sub-page, permission rules, error cases, and any DB schema changes. If UI behaviour drives the API, attach the UI spec or mock for the popup.

explanation: The story states that several fields should 'already be handled' and references conditional visibility based on request settings, but it does not enumerate which fields (beyond 'Response Comment') nor the exact conditions/rules that govern their visibility or editability. This prevents a reliable estimate.
suggested_fix: List every field to be shown in 'My Response', define the triggering request settings/params that control each field's visibility and editability, and provide acceptance criteria with examples (including failure/edge cases). Add UI mocks or Gherkin scenarios where possible.

explanation: The story asks to 'support the same apply processing logic' plus 'additional validation checks' and to determine resulting actions (Complete Request/Stage) based on varied request staging conditions. Those 'additional validations' and exact mapping rules are not specified, leaving scope ambiguous.
suggested_fix: Clarify and list the specific validation rules to add, provide a decision table that maps request/stage states to the expected Complete Request and Stage actions, and reference the existing Grouped Review implementation (or include example scenarios). Break into smaller stories if necessary (e.g., core apply logic vs. each new validation).

explanation: Acceptance language is vague and hedged (e.g., 'probably', 'may show', 'controls may be hidden', 'MP4 may autoplay') so the expected user-visible behavior and edge cases are unclear. Without deterministic criteria (when preview appears, whether controls are shown, autoplay rules, behavior if conversion is pending or fails), the team cannot reliably estimate effort or testing required.
suggested_fix: Clarify acceptance criteria with concrete, testable behaviors: e.g., 'When a GIF/Lottie is converted to MP4 and the user opens the Share screen, show the converted MP4 preview in a playback component. Controls: visible/hidden (specify). Autoplay: on/off (specify) and whether muted. Define behavior when conversion is pending or failed (placeholder, error message). Specify browser support, maximum duration/file-size rules, and accessibility requirements. Add example Gherkin scenarios and edge cases.

explanation: [NOT_INDEPENDENT] IB2-326 (Ask button behaviour for Main Admins) explicitly requires the AI Assist visibility / Main Admin access restriction (IB2-318) and the 'Limit to current view' search-scope logic (IB2-299) to be available so the Ask button can be shown only to Main Admins and use the checkbox state. It also depends on the Ask button UI/behaviour defined in IB2-323. Without IB2-318 and IB2-299 (and the Ask button UI from IB2-323) the acceptance criteria for IB2-326 cannot be validated: the button visibility, permission gating and correct search-scope behaviour cannot be tested or delivered independently.
suggested_fix: Implement the prerequisites (IB2-318 and IB2-299) and the Ask-button UI (IB2-323) before or in the same coordinated sprint as IB2-326. Alternatively, split IB2-326 into two parts: (A) gate/permission + visibility (depends on IB2-318 + IB2-323) and (B) the search-scope integration (depends on IB2-299), or provide a test stub/mocked permission and checkbox to allow independent testing if delivering prerequisites first is not possible.

explanation: [NOT_INDEPENDENT] IB2-366 (Guest Upload MVP: randomized success confirmation presentation) explicitly states it should 'reuse the same public-user page header layout' and notes it 'should wait until IB-382 completed'. IB2-382 changes the public-user header by hiding the database name and close icon for public users. Because IB2-366's acceptance depends on that simplified public header being present, it cannot be fully implemented, tested, or accepted independently until IB2-382 is completed.
suggested_fix: Schedule IB2-382 (public-user header simplification) before IB2-366, or split IB2-366 so the randomized presentation work is implemented in a way that does not rely on IB2-382 (e.g., include a local simplified-header fallback variant for testing) to allow independent delivery.

explanation: This single story bundles many distinct deliverables across multiple areas (MCP setting + toggle behavior, provider switch from Imagga to Azure, migration of Imagga configs and usage history, billing & quota tracking updates, analytics/reporting, notifications, API/permissions updates, docs and rollout). These are separate user goals and cross-cutting concerns that will not fit in a single sprint as a single story.
suggested_fix: Split into smaller, independently valuable stories/epics. For example: (1) Add MCP setting and toggle to prefer Azure image tagging and wire basic provider-switch behavior; (2) Add Azure Image Processing Limit setting and rename/update video limit units; (3) Data migration epic for Imagga config & usage history with acceptance criteria and rollback plan; (4) Billing & quota tracking updates with measurable metrics; (5) Analytics & provider activity reporting; (6) Notifications for limits/quota exhaustion/provider failures; (7) API/permissions/documentation and rollout plan. For each child story add concrete acceptance criteria, NFR targets (e.g., migration data mapping, expected processing latencies, quota limits), and test cases.

explanation: The scope includes multiple large tasks (schema/report renaming, filtering out dev servers, historical data migration, multi-environment aggregation, backward compatibility and automated sync). Together these are broad and likely span multiple sprints/multiple teams.
suggested_fix: Split into smaller stories/epics: (a) Update weekly report schema and rename (incl. tests), (b) Implement dev-server filtering and deployment config, (c) Historical data migration plan and migration job, (d) Multi-environment aggregation and export sync (could be its own epic). For each child story include data volume estimates, acceptance tests, and rollback strategy.

explanation: This story bundles many large, distinct capabilities (drag-and-drop move, child reference migration, URL and SEO updates, undo/redo and full version history, audit logging, notifications, permission/approval workflow changes, cross-API/mobile/collab synchronization). Together these represent multiple vertical features and cross-cutting concerns—it reads like an epic rather than a single sprint-sized story.
suggested_fix: Split into smaller, independently deliverable stories. Suggested decomposition: (1) Core move operation with child-preservation and persisted navigation order, (2) URL/breadcrumb/SEO update story, (3) Data migration for child references and dependency reconciliation, (4) Permissions & workflow adjustments, (5) Undo/redo and version history, (6) Audit logging & notifications, (7) Cross-client/API synchronization and compatibility checks. For each split story include precise acceptance criteria and tests.

explanation: The story bundles many distinct, large capabilities (view/configure sub-pages, permission propagation, hierarchical inheritance across multiple levels, approval workflows, audit history, cross-platform synchronization). This is an epic-sized scope that cannot reasonably be completed as a single story within a sprint.
suggested_fix: Split into smaller, vertical stories. Examples: (1) UI to view/configure sub-pages in Admin→Group; (2) implement one-level permission propagation with acceptance tests; (3) extend to multi-level inheritance; (4) add audit history for permission changes; (5) add synchronization/interop with other platforms. For each, add clear acceptance criteria and NFRs (security, performance).

explanation: The requirements list many broad, cross-cutting features (global template inheritance, multilingual generation, tenant-level customisation, real-time delivery tracking, approval workflows, external sync). Combined these form a large, multi-functional scope that should be broken down.
suggested_fix: Decompose into smaller stories: core support for grouped notifications in Resource Publish Templates (baseline UI/flags), then separate stories for multilingual templating, tenant customisation, delivery tracking, and external provider synchronization. For each, supply testable acceptance criteria and data/format examples.

explanation: The card is phrased as a question/clarification rather than a deliverable user story. It lacks a clear actor, expected outcome, and measurable user/business value.
suggested_fix: Reword into a proper user story: state who needs the information, why it delivers value, and what the expected acceptance criteria are. Example: 'As a Page Builder, I want documentation and in-UI help that explains the combined Block and Widget settings so I can configure widgets correctly' with acceptance criteria listing the specific settings to document and where the help text appears.
